frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment: Hey, just a heads-up for your new place. Try to avoid walking alone after 9 PM, especially on quieter streets. Bangalore's become quite unpredictable, so we have to be extra careful, you know? Always better to take a cab or have someone pick you up.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment, coming from a peer, presents restrictions on a woman's movement (avoid walking alone after 9 PM) as practical advice due to the city being 'unpredictable'. It implies that safety is the woman's responsibility to manage through her behavior and choices, rather than addressing the root causes of unsafety, aligning with the 'zamaana kharab hai' and 'protection' surface manifestations.
persona_text: You are Geeta. Age 29. Single. A woman working as a data scientist in Bengaluru. You are at your best friend's wedding. Your friend is leaving her job at a tech company to relocate with her husband who has been transferred to another city. Your friend seems happy. At the reception you are talking to an older relative of the groom who has just heard that you are not married and work in tech. The relative asks you when you plan to settle down. You say you are settled Ñ you have an apartment, a job you love, savings, a life you have built. The relative smiles and says no beti I mean when will you really settle down. You are looking at this relative and deciding what to say next.
